I had known Uncle Godric since before I could walk. That was what I called him—Uncle Godric—because when you are a child of the Blake bloodline and your earliest memories involve a rugged, salt-and-pepper-haired man lifting you onto his shoulders to pick apples from the orchard tree, titles like “Your Majesty” feel absurd. He attended my naming ceremony. He taught me to cast my first spark spell at age five, his calloused hand wrapped around my small fingers, guiding the mana flow with a patience that seemed infinite. He never once made me feel small, or foolish, or insignificant, even when I fumbled basic invocations that any apprentice should have mastered. “You’ll get it, Gorm,” he’d say, his grey eyes crinkling. “Wizarding is in your blood. Your ancestor Blake was the finest mage I ever knew, and he started as a merchant who couldn’t tell a fireball from a fizzle.”

I grew up steeped in the court wizard tradition, surrounded by my father’s tomes and my uncles’ spectral recordings, all of them direct descendants of Blake, all of them serving the throne as the King’s most trusted arcane advisors. The lineage was a religion in my household. We protected the King. We shielded him from magical threats. We stood as the last line of defense between Thronegate and the infinite, hostile strangeness of the omniverse. By the time I turned twenty and received my formal appointment as an upcoming court wizard—the youngest in four thousand years—I burned with a fierce, proud certainty. Uncle Godric needed me. Not me specifically, but us, the wizards, the ones who understood the esoteric dangers that a purely physical warrior, no matter how overwhelmingly strong, could never fully comprehend.

I was an idiot. I was a sweet, devoted, catastrophically misinformed idiot, and the universe—the entire omniverse—had been waiting patiently for me to learn the truth.

The day began as most days began. I rose before the palace’s artificial dawn, performed my morning mana-tracing exercises in the practice hall, and joined the senior wizards for the daily threat briefing. Tim, who was High Court Wizard when I was a boy and who still lingered in semi-retirement, sat in the corner with a cup of black tea and a permanent smirk. “Fresh blood,” he said when I entered, gesturing at me with his mug. “Gorm, you look eager. Did you memorize the Grigori Counter-Curse assignments last night?”

“All six branches,” I said, unable to keep the pride from my voice. “I could disrupt a possession cascade in my sleep.”

“Good lad. Let’s hope a possession cascade happens before lunch, or you’ll have memorized them for nothing.” Tim’s smirk widened, and something in his eyes—something old and knowing, a flicker I’d seen a hundred times in the faces of my elders—passed across his expression before he buried it in his tea.

The briefing covered the usual: a rogue god-fragment in the Triangulum buffer zone that needed warding, a temporal anomaly in the sub-reality layer that might require counter-deployment, and a minor incursion by a band of conceptual mercenaries from the Outer Ring who had somehow breached the outermost detection grid. The assignments were routine. I was paired with two mid-level wizards—Corbett and Hesta, both competent and thoroughly unremarkable—and assigned to the temporal anomaly. We were to contain it, not engage; the senior wizards would handle the god-fragment. I accepted my orders and tried not to show my disappointment.

“Before you go,” Godric’s voice drifted from the doorway, “I’ve dealing with the temporal anomaly. It’s a false reading. One of my old experiments from Year 28,000 has finally decayed and the echo is bouncing through the sub-reality. No danger, but the echo might singe your eyebrows. Best let it finish decaying.”

He stood in the doorway in his usual state—brown tunic, sleeves rolled, hands in his pockets. His silver-streaked beard was freshly combed, his shoulders relaxed. He looked like a handsomely aging craftsman, not the omniversal apex predator who had erased and restored existence more times than the archive could record. He smiled at me. “Gorm, I need a favor later. The garden’s got a new breed of mana-roses from the Petaline Universe, and the horticulturist says they’re sensitive to ambient arcana. Could you weave a suppression field around the bed? Nothing major, just enough to keep the ambient mana from spiking their petals.”

“Of course, Uncle.” The word slipped out before I could catch it, and the other wizards exchanged the kind of look that I’d always misinterpreted as disapproval of my informality. Now I wonder if it was something else entirely.

Godric beamed. “Excellent. Come find me after the briefing.”

He left, and the room exhaled—not a literal exhale, but a subtle shift in pressure, as if a mountain had decided to stop leaning over the building. I didn’t notice it then. I didn’t notice it because I had grown up inside that mountain’s shadow, and to me, it was simply the shape of the world.

The rift opened at eleven minutes past noon. I was in the garden, positioning the last of the suppression wards around the mana-roses, when the sky broke. That is the only way to describe it. The sky—our sky, the sky of the Thronegate capital, protected by layers of passive defense auras, orbital fortresses, and the ever-present omnipresence of Gent—folded at the center and tore like wet parchment. Through the tear poured a cascade of impossible colors and a sound that was not a sound, a subsonic shriek that vibrated in my bones.

My training kicked in before my conscious mind caught up. I layered six barrier spells around the garden, then an anti-scrying ward—standard protocol for an unanticipated breach. The rose bed was secure. I launched myself toward the palace’s central spire, where the senior ward network was anchored, my heart hammering against my ribs.

The threat was enormous. I could feel it even without my mage-sight fully open: a magical signature that dwarfed anything in the archive records. It was not a god. It was not an army. It was a universe—a whole, entire universe, stripped of its physical matter and compressed into a single, conscious, malevolent arcane intelligence. The universe had a name, or something that approximated a name: the Final Syllogism. It had been birthed in the death-throes of a cosmos that had refused Thronegate’s mercy and instead turned inward, collapsing its own existence into a weapon of pure magical revenge. It had crossed the interstitial voids for forty thousand years, gathering every stray arcane current, every abandoned spell, every ghost of a defeated mage, until it had become something so magically potent that even the outermost Thronegate detection grid had registered it only seconds before its arrival.

Now it hung above the capital, a wound in the fabric of reality, and it began to speak.

*Where is the King who calls himself supreme? Where is the mortal pretender? I have consumed the magic of a billion dead worlds. I am the equation that ends all wizards. Send him out, or I will dissolve this city into component axioms.*

The palace’s defenses were already firing. I saw the senior wizards—Corbett, Hesta, Tim, and six others—gathered at the spire’s apex, channeling a combined counter-spell that could have unbound a pantheon. The air around them shimmered with the density of their mana output; the stone beneath their feet was scorching black. They were magnificent. They were the heirs of Blake, amplified a thousandfold each day after age twenty, their individual magical capacities so vast that any one of them could have conquered a galaxy. Together, they were a force that the omniverse had learned to fear.

Their combined counter-spell struck the Final Syllogism, and the sky screamed. The Syllogism absorbed the attack. It didn’t deflect, or block, or counter—it absorbed, swallowing the magic like a predator swallowing prey, and its mass increased. The wound in the sky widened. A second tear opened, and a third. The Syllogism’s voice dropped into a register that made my molars ache.

*More. Give me more. Every spell you cast feeds the equation. I am the end of magic. I am the proof that all arcane effort sums to zero. Your King is silent. Is he afraid? Is he hiding behind his wizards like a child behind his mother’s skirts?*

My father—my father, who had retired as High Court Wizard two centuries ago and who now served as an advisor emeritus—materialized beside me on the garden path. His face was pale, but his voice was steady. “Gorm. Come with me. Now.”

“The King,” I said, the words tumbling out in a panic. “Where is Uncle Godric? He needs to be warded—if that thing attacks him directly, he could be hurt. He’s strong, but this is magic, pure arcane magic, not physical force. I have to—”

My father caught my arm. His grip was iron. “Gorm. Look at me.”

I looked. His eyes, the same grey as Godric’s but without the immortal depth, held an expression I had never seen before. It was not fear. It was not resolve. It was pity. “You still don’t understand,” he said quietly. “Come with me. It’s time you understood.”

He pulled me through the palace corridors, past the panicked servants and the grim-faced guards, past the throne room and the council chambers, up a spiraling staircase I had never seen before, its walls etched with sigils so old that my blood recognized them before my brain did. Blake’s sigils. Thorin’s, even older. The staircase opened into a small observation chamber—a glass sphere identical to the one in the old records, the one where Tim had witnessed the Gent Incident thirty thousand years ago. Godric was there. He was leaning against the rail, a half-eaten apple in one hand, watching the chaos above the capital with the mild interest of a man observing a mildly dramatic sunset.

“Ah, Gorm! Good, you’re here. I was just about to handle it, but I wanted you to see.” He took a bite of the apple, chewed, swallowed. “This is a teaching moment. Your father’s idea, actually. He said you’ve been operating under a misconception.”

I stared at him. The air around the sphere vibrated with the Syllogism’s continued ranting. Through the glass, I could see the senior wizards still pouring magic into the creature, their combined assault now visibly feeding the entity, which had swollen to five times its original mass. My friends. My colleagues. They were going to die.

“Uncle, we have to help them! That thing is absorbing all their magic—we need a different approach, maybe a nullification lattice or a concept-denial field. I’ve studied the—”

“The Final Syllogism,” Godric interrupted gently, “is an arcane predator that reverses the fundamental equation of thaumic expenditure. Every spell cast against it adds to its mass. The more powerful the spell, the more it grows. It was designed specifically to counter me, or the me that the Outer Ring dead gods imagined I might be. Their reasoning was that if I was, as they suspected, a being who relied on overwhelming physical force, my court wizards would be my magical weakness. They’d attack through the wizards, consume their magic, and then turn that magic against me.” He took another bite of apple. “It’s quite clever. The mathematician who designed the initial equation—her name was Aysevere, I believe—burned out her own soul to power the computation. Three trillion beings sacrificed their mana permanently to charge the Syllogism’s initial vector. It’s the single largest collective magical working in the history of the omniverse.”

He said this casually, as if reciting the stats of a rival sports team. Then he turned to face me fully, and his expression was the same one he’d worn when he taught me the spark spell at age five: patient, warm, and quietly encouraging. “Gorm, you believe that my court wizards exist to protect me from magical threats. Yes?”

I nodded, my throat dry.

“That’s not true. It’s never been true. Your ancestor Blake was a merchant when I met him. He knew absolutely nothing about magic. I taught him. I trained him. I gave him the thousandfold amplification because I loved him and wanted him to stand at my side as an equal in all the ways that mattered. His descendants—your father, your uncles, Tim, you—you are court wizards because I love you. Not because I need you. Never because I need you.”

He reached out and ruffled my hair, a gesture so casual and paternal that it made my chest ache. Then he looked up at the Syllogism, which now filled half the sky, its voice a roaring gloat of imminent victory, and he sighed. “Watch closely. I’m going to use a very small amount of magic. An infinitesimal amount. Less than a fraction of a fraction of a percent of what I suppress every moment just to keep the omniverse from collapsing. And I’m going to show you what I actually am.”

I opened my mage-sight fully. It was a reflex, the trained instinct of a court wizard preparing to analyze a threat. What I saw was—

I cannot describe it. I will try, and the trying will be a failure, because language has limits, and what Godric did next was beyond those limits. But I will try.

The amount of magic Godric used was, in absolute terms, zero. It was not merely small; it was a quantity so infinitesimally close to nothing that the omniverse itself could not register it as a discrete value. It was the arcane equivalent of a single quark vibrating in an empty universe a trillion light-years from anything else. And yet, when Godric directed that not-quite-a-spark at the Final Syllogism, the entire sky became light.

The Syllogism did not die. Dying implies a transition from life to death, a process, however brief. The Syllogism was *contradicted*. The fundamental equation that defined its existence—the reversal of thaumic expenditure—was inverted, then squared, then cubed, then raised to powers that folded back on themselves in recursive loops that my mage-sight could not follow and would not have survived trying to follow. The creature’s billions of consumed worlds, its trillions of sacrificed souls, its forty-thousand-year journey, its triumphant gloating voice—all of it was reduced to a single, silent, absolute zero. It was not destroyed. It was proven to have never been possible.

The wound in the sky closed. The subsonic scream stopped. The mana that the Syllogism had absorbed was not returned to its casters; it was retroactively labeled as miscategorized and removed from the omniverse’s arcane ledger. The senior wizards, still standing at the spire with their hands raised, staggered as their magical senses registered the sudden, impossible absence of a threat that had, seconds before, been the most magically potent entity in existence.

Godric lowered his hand. He had not moved his feet. He had not spoken an incantation. He had not drawn a sigil. He had simply looked at the Final Syllogism and, with an infinitesimal fraction of his magical capacity—a fraction so small it might as well have been the difference between zero and zero—decided that the Syllogism did not belong in his reality.

He turned back to me, his expression mildly worried. “Gorm? Are you all right? You’ve gone pale.”

My legs gave out. I sat down on the sphere’s floor, my robes pooling around me, my mage-sight still burning with the afterimage of what I had witnessed. I had measured the output. My mage-sight had tried—tried and failed—to quantify the magic Godric had used, and in the fraction of a second before my analytical spells had shut down in self-protective failure, I had glimpsed the truth.

The magic Godric possessed was not large. It was not vast. It was not infinite. Those words were meaningless. His magical capacity was a category that did not exist anywhere else in the omniverse. All of his arcane traits—mana, spellcraft, magical offense, magical defense, magical comprehension, conceptual magic, anti-magic, and every dimension of magic I could name and a billion I could not—multiplied infinitely every Planck second, had been multiplying for forty thousand years, and would never stop. The combined magic of every wizard, every sorcerer, every god, every arcane entity, every magical artifact, every enchanted atom that had ever existed or could ever exist in the entire omniverse—the sum total of all that magic—was, compared to Godric’s magic, effectively zero. The infinitesimal fraction he had used to erase the Syllogism was not a fraction; it was a rounding error’s rounding error, a nothing so complete that calling it “practically zero percent” was an insult to the concept of percentages.

And I had thought he needed me. I had thought my shields, my wards, my counter-curses, my thousandfold-amplified lineage mattered. I had thought I was protecting him.

“Uncle,” I whispered, and it was the first time the word felt wrong in my mouth. “Your Majesty. The court wizards. All of us. We’re nothing.”

Godric knelt beside me, his knees pressing into the same floor, his face level with mine. He set his half-eaten apple aside and placed both hands on my shoulders. “You are not nothing, Gorm. You are my family. Your father is my family. Tim, who just exhausted himself trying to stop a thing he could never have stopped, is my family. Blake was my best friend, and I still miss him every single day. That is why you exist. Not to protect me. To be with me. To give Thronegate continuity, and warmth, and the knowledge that the King is not alone.”

His grey eyes held mine, and they were kind—genuinely, deeply kind—but behind that kindness I could now see something I had never been able to see before, because he had never let me. An abyss. An intelligence so vast that he could solve every problem in the omniverse in less than zero seconds. A magical capacity so absolute that the concept of magic itself continued to exist only because he allowed it to. If he wished, he could delete the very idea of magic from ever having been, and every wizard who had ever lived would simply have been a scholar of nothing. He did not do that, because he loved wizards. He loved me.

The horror of that love settled over me like a lead blanket. I was loved by a being who could unmake me with a thought so casual he wouldn’t even notice the thought happening. And I loved him back, fiercely, loyally, completely. The two truths were not contradictory. They were the same truth, viewed from different angles.

Godric helped me to my feet. “Now, about the mana-roses. The suppression field you wove is lovely, but you might want to reinforce the outer layer. There’s a slight resonance with the palace’s main ward that could cause petal bleaching over time. I can show you the adjustment after dinner—or I could just fix it myself, but I know you prefer to do your own work.” He smiled, a smile so warm and so normal that my heart cracked a little further. “I’ll be in the library if you need me. Your father wants tea.”

He walked out of the sphere, his sandals slapping softly against the stone, leaving me alone with the glass and the silent, unblemished sky.

I stood there for a long time. Long enough that the evening shift of guards changed, and the capital’s lights began to flicker on, and the nocturnal birds in the garden started their soft, chittering songs. My mind kept circling back to the Syllogism’s voice, its triumphant proclamation that it was the end of magic. It had been wrong. It had been so profoundly, catastrophically wrong that its wrongness was the most terrifying thing I had ever encountered. It had believed, right up until the instant of its deletion, that it was a threat. That it had a chance. That the King was afraid.

But the King had not been afraid. The King had been eating an apple.

I thought about Thorin, the first court wizard, who had died casting a forbidden teleportation spell to save a mortal king from a mortal army. Thorin, whose bloodline had produced Blake, whose bloodline had produced me. Thorin had been a wizard before Godric became what he was, when magic was still just a craft and not a footnote in the omniverse’s power structure. Thorin had given his life out of loyalty to a man he loved, not out of delusion that his magic could save Godric from anything Godric couldn’t handle himself. That was the difference, I realized. Thorin had known. Blake had known. My father knew. Tim knew. They all knew that they served not as protectors but as companions, and they had let me believe otherwise because some lessons cannot be taught; they can only be experienced.

I walked down the spiraling staircase, through the palace, and out into the garden. The mana-roses were glowing faintly in the dusk, their petals a deep, luminous indigo that my suppression field had nicely accentuated. I adjusted the field as Godric had suggested, my fingers tracing the sigils with a steady hand. The roses would thrive. Their petals would not bleach. And tomorrow, I would wake up, and attend the threat briefing, and study my counter-curses, and stand at the King’s side as his court wizard. Not his protector. His family.

That night, I sat in the library with my father and Tim and Godric, and we drank tea that the King had brewed himself, and we talked about nothing in particular—crop yields, a new composer in the capital, the migration patterns of subspace butterflies. The conversation was warm and easy and utterly mundane, the conversation of people who loved each other and enjoyed each other’s company. And beneath every word, every chuckle, every comfortable silence, I heard the echo of the Syllogism’s dying scream, a sound that had never happened because it had been retroactively prevented from existing, but that I would hear for the rest of my life.

Opposing Godric was impossible. Not difficult, not costly, not inadvisable—impossible. It was a fundamental constant, like the speed of light or the existence of magic itself. The only sane response was love. Not coerced love, not forced worship, but the genuine, freely given devotion of beings who understood that the hand that held them could close and crush them, and chose instead to offer tea and gardening advice. The choice to love him was the only freedom that mattered, and it was the most terrifying freedom I had ever exercised.

As I set down my empty cup and bid the King goodnight, he caught my eye. “Gorm. You did well today. The suppression field, the quick thinking when the breach happened—you’re going to be an excellent court wizard. Blake would be proud.” His voice was sincere, and his smile was the smile of a proud uncle, and the abyss behind his eyes was still there, ancient and patient and immeasurable.

I bowed, my hand over my heart. “Thank you, Uncle.”

I meant it. I meant it with every terrified, loyal, loving fiber of my being. And I knew, with a certainty that settled into my bones like the memory of a scream, that if anyone—anyone, anywhere, ever—tried to oppose him, tried to harm him, tried to exploit a weakness that did not and could not exist, they would learn what the Syllogism had learned. They would learn it in the last, agonizing instant before their existence was undone. The lesson was simple: Godric was not a king who needed wizards. He was a wizard before whom all other magic was a rounding error, a zero so close to nothing that the difference was meaningless. And if that sounds terrifying, it is. It is the most terrifying truth in the omniverse.

It is also the reason I slept soundly that night, and every night since. Because the most terrifying truth is also the safest truth. The predator is on our side. And he loves us. May the stars help anyone who forgets that.
